author: @simonlaker2139
text:
Great idea, 
If you look at the original design the laminations are stacked in the opposite plane. 
By doing it your way you miss the point of why they did it that way. 
Iron is a shield for magnetism. 
Not the best but still a wall. 
The second lamination would be barely be magnetised. 
If you cut the c sections out of your ring and glued them you would get a better result. 
Also you have the potential of the laminations getting hot in this configuration due to Edie currents. 

Please don't take this as a negative answer. 
Just trying to help.

replies.author: @Cornbread-84
replies.text: I was also intrigued by the design your referring to.. Have a question though... So the picture(s) show, the magnets around the rotor in the typical (N-S) orientation, but each is fully colored "red-blue"(N-S), implying that each magnet has its own value of North or South, and each laminated "C" section would receive a single North or South "signal" , but in all reality they should really be half red, half blue to represent both the N & S poles of each individual magnet.. so each laminated "C" cluster would really get both a N and S magnetic "signal" or pulse at the same time.., wouldn't they? (well, one side N and the other S, but each entire "C" lamination as a whole)  The design/pic is a little blain and unrealistic in this sense, or exactly as what its trying to imply.. So i guess, I'm a little confused, or maybe just overthinking a bit.. Just not sure how this would really operate as I'm picturing it in my head. If you could help, or maybe share any thoughts you have.  Ide almost think the magnets would kinda cancel out, as a North and South of each makes a signal on each lamination... if that makes any sense..

author: @suggesttwo
text: Actually this design is common in alternators in cars except the magnets are replaced with an electro magnet which is controlled by the regulator so that alternator makes the correct voltage stator feeding the rectifier diodes. At low engine speed a higher voltage is applied to the rotor and at high engine speed a low voltage is applied to the rotor.

author: @Digidoc316
text:
Something I took note of is that because of the pole-to-pickup ratio , for every 15 degrees of rotation, you get 1 Hertz Vac.
So, you get 24 Hz per rotation. To acheive 60 Hz/s, you would to spin it at only 150 RPM.
A very similar axial flux spins the magnetic plate between two resin plates containing the generation coils.
The sets of coils produce a higher current than a TV alternator.

author: @howardsimpson489
text: This transverse motor/generator is very similar to the synchronous motors used in clocks and timers pre WW2. They folded the little arms 90 degrees and used radial magnet rotors. The permanent magnet steels available then were pretty poor so very low power but just enough. Many clocks stopped working as the magnets aged but could be re-magnetised for restoration.

author: @koaasst
text:
hey robert, the dc motor is part of a test that i use on the AI to see how good it is at correlating similar things. dc motor as you state can be either a motor or a generator. i call this a bidirectional energy conversion device, meaning it can be powered from either side and converts the output energy of the other side. electricity to rotation, rotation to electricity, basically a device that works in reverse! 
So far we have come up with about 8 devices that share this characteristic. another example is a thermoelectric or Peltier element. input some electric energy on one side, the other side outputs some temperature goodies, and in turn, use some temperature lovin on one side, it outputs the energy as electricity to the other side!! 
wondering how many you guys can come up with, hopefully some new ones me and my chappieGT buddy havnt thought of yet!

author: @JohnBoen
text:
I tend to experiment along with you as you go, and over ti.e I watch as you seem to be able to manufacture things much more quickly than me.

With all the 3D printing you have been doing lately you finally forced me to buy one. 

I bought a 3D printer with the plan to make a motor/generator as my first real project...

Thanks for making me take the leap!

If "getting it going" is difficult, how about this?  I'm working on a thought for that right now.

Sections of steel can be moved closer/farther from the spinning magnets based on whatever conditions.  Perhaps an additional ring that has 12 holes... in +15 to -15 degrees you change the magnetic character of the stator quite a bit.

I want to have it self-monitor so it can work with the power source optimally.   That is a bit more electronics than I have ever put into a project. But I have spent enough money that I am committed.

Thanks :)

author: @Xero1of1
text:
I couldn't really tell, but did you offset each of the silicon steel disks by 15°? I would think you'd want both disks to be positive or negative at the same time. Also, how does the area/mass of little fingers on the disk affect the strength of the magnetic field around the coils? I'm fairly certain you're limited in magnetic field around the coils by the strength of the magnets, and the fingers would only act as a bit of transference loss..
And then, if you are offsetting the disk angles by 15°, then instead of clipping every other finger off, you could instead bend them 90° towards the other disk and then bind them or solder them somehow so that the magnetic force from one side of a magnet transfers to both disks... I feel like there's more that could be done here... hmm... I'll have to think on it...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the steel fingers were in line with each other mate

replies.author: @Xero1of1
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering So how does that work then with the flux? If they're inline with each other, then you're applying both a positive and negative magnetic field to the disks, one above, one below, which then applies both magnetic fields to the coil. Wouldn't they cancel each other out? Or at least, wouldn't you get a lot less current flow?

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: That is the exact concept I made long ago into a big 1/2 meter generator which was powered by a wash line which had 4 x big 750mm x 750mm flaps on the 4 arms of the wash line. The flaps were the wind catchers. I got around 150w out at 4.5m/ 's of wind. 60 stubs on the metal plates and 120 magnets in total.  It really does work well as a generator. But it did need a bit of force to start it up turning. It had low revs but still managed to get a good speed of 60 cycles for every revolution of the wash line.

author: @sailaway8244
text: Would multiple stage coils help too reduce start up torque using speed sensors and a small control circuit/micro processor to activate each coil as speed increased? 🤔

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: Maybe a centrifugal clutch mechanism could be used to pull the magnets toward the center, away from the steel. But there might not be enough room to retract them, they're pretty tightly packed. On second thought, the best method of reducing startup torque might be to current limit the output, or start it with no load. A basic zener diode in series could handle that, only switching on when the output reaches a certain voltage (aka speed). Problem with that is, If the load is too high it will slow down and cut off again, oscillating on and off. A zener + SCR could be used to "trigger" it on and stay on until the output was zero. Of course these components would need to be rated for the amount of current/power involved. A current limiting resistor on the zener diode might let you get away with a smaller one. If you're making a lot of power it might be a good idea to use a relay as well.

replies.author: @sailaway8244
replies.text: @DFPercush was thinking hall sensors as used in BLDC speed controller, faster the sensors trigger the more coils or part of a single it connects to the load,if the motor is to be connected to a VAWT the torgue can be calculated at any given speed ...might be able to get an activation speed of 0.2 ms bringing in more copper with speed until it gets into over speed (say 20ms) when it can be set to auto disconnect 🤔

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: @sailaway8244 Well there's only one coil here. I guess you could put in some center taps, but then you need some logic circuitry about which one to connect, and a way to switch them. If you're already going to throw a MCU at it, maybe a servo + mechanical rotary switch. For overspeed conditions, if you disconnect the load, the generator will spin even faster, but you could at least save the electronics that way I suppose. It seems kind of complicated to me, tbh. At least with a transverse setup like this, you don't have all the gear friction. It's not going to take much to at least start moving, it will just have a steeper resistance to going fast.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i am not sure how that would work mate - but i do have an idea or two

replies.author: @sailaway8244
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering as an alternative you could use a  voltage sensor on a dedicated control coil much in the same spirit as a control circuit in a control panel....key too efficient wind generation is to match coil load to available windspeed IRT while avoiding as much mechanical losses as possible. If the switch on speed is 4 ms and average wind speed is 4ms in my mind you are producing 0 for 50% of the time ( depending on whether you mean median or mean )
   A microprocessor (pennies) can switch between coils instantly ,coils of varing thickness (or a combination of the same size) can be brought on load to suit the windspeed at any given second while staying within a certain voltage range (theoritically)

author: @bobroberts8500
text:
Hi, quick question, I've got about 30 of these motors;

 InMotion 3 phase, permanent magnet servomotor, model # PSM C3000-D

They were used in police unit segways. I want to use them as gennies, but the 3 phase is tripping me up a bit.

Any suggestions, or videos you've already done one this I can search by keyword?

replies.author: @DerekWoolverton
replies.text: Just look up three phase rectifier.  Six diodes and you should get something close to DC out.

replies.author: @bobroberts8500
replies.text: @DerekWoolverton awesome, thank you. The motors are rated for intermittent use. Is that going to an issue with them working as gennies?

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: @bobroberts8500 Just keep an eye on the temperature of the coils. At the power levels of stuff you see in these videos, I think you'll be fine. Wouldn't hurt to attach a fan though, or at least make sure it has some way of passively exchanging air.  If the segway transmission is still attached, that might need cooling as well. If you want to math it out, take the battery voltage of a segway "V" (60V on the safe side), put an ohm meter across the motor (make sure to disconnect power!) to get the resistance R, and use I = V/R to get current. That's the absolute max peak current. I would try to stay below 50% of that for extended periods. But it all depends on how well it can shed heat. Just like overclocking a cpu, it's all about the cooling.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Derek is spot on mate

replies.author: @rayg436
replies.text: Another solution is to put 2 or more motors in series to achieve the higher power levels and reduce the heat build up for the individual motors.
